quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Monosaccharide"?

options.text: 5' cap (7-methylguanosine) and 3' poly-A tail added for stability and export; introns spliced out by spliceosomes
A simple sugar (glucose, fructose, galactose) with the general formula (CH₂O)n; the monomer of carbohydrates
A measure of disorder or randomness; the 2nd law of thermodynamics states that entropy of the universe increases in spontaneous processes
ATP production driven by the proton gradient across the thylakoid membrane; H⁺ flows through ATP synthase from lumen to stroma

quiz_questions.explanation: "Monosaccharide" is defined as: A simple sugar (glucose, fructose, galactose) with the general formula (CH₂O)n; the monomer of carbohydrates. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Glycosidic bond"?

options.text: ATP production driven by the proton gradient across the thylakoid membrane; H⁺ flows through ATP synthase from lumen to stroma
A covalent bond formed between two sugar molecules by dehydration synthesis; α or β orientation determines the polysaccharide's properties
The variety of life at three levels: genetic diversity (within species), species diversity (number and relative abundance), and ecosystem diversity
Multiple genes contribute to a single trait, producing continuous variation (e.g., skin color, height, intelligence)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Glycosidic bond" is defined as: A covalent bond formed between two sugar molecules by dehydration synthesis; α or β orientation determines the polysaccharide's properties. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Starch"?

options.text: A storage polysaccharide in plants made of α-glucose linked by α-1,4 (amylose) and α-1,4/α-1,6 (amylopectin) glycosidic bonds
A gene encoding a protein that inhibits cell division (e.g., p53 triggers apoptosis in damaged cells, Rb blocks G1/S transition)
Benefits humans receive from ecosystems: provisioning (food, water), regulating (climate, flood control), cultural (recreation), and supporting (nutrient cycling, soil formation)
A 3-carbon sugar produced by the Calvin cycle; the direct product used to build glucose, amino acids, and fatty acids

quiz_questions.explanation: "Starch" is defined as: A storage polysaccharide in plants made of α-glucose linked by α-1,4 (amylose) and α-1,4/α-1,6 (amylopectin) glycosidic bonds. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Cellulose"?

options.text: 5' cap (7-methylguanosine) and 3' poly-A tail added for stability and export; introns spliced out by spliceosomes
Failure of chromosomes to separate properly during meiosis, resulting in gametes with abnormal chromosome numbers (aneuploidy)
The main replication enzyme in E. coli; synthesizes DNA 5'→3' by adding nucleotides complementary to the template strand
A structural polysaccharide in plant cell walls made of β-glucose linked by β-1,4 glycosidic bonds; forms rigid, indigestible fibers

quiz_questions.explanation: "Cellulose" is defined as: A structural polysaccharide in plant cell walls made of β-glucose linked by β-1,4 glycosidic bonds; forms rigid, indigestible fibers. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Chitin"?

options.text: A series of molecular events by which a signal on a cell's surface is converted to a specific cellular response
A structural polysaccharide in arthropod exoskeletons and fungal cell walls; similar to cellulose but with nitrogen-containing groups
The monomer of proteins: a central carbon bonded to an amino group (-NH₂), carboxyl group (-COOH), hydrogen, and variable R-group
A phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins that controls what enters and exits the cell; selectively permeable

quiz_questions.explanation: "Chitin" is defined as: A structural polysaccharide in arthropod exoskeletons and fungal cell walls; similar to cellulose but with nitrogen-containing groups. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A simple sugar (glucose, fructose, galactose) with the general formula (CH₂O)n; the monomer of carbohydrates"?

options.text: Vestigial structures
Monosaccharide
Nucleus
Species richness

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Monosaccharide".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A covalent bond formed between two sugar molecules by dehydration synthesis; α or β orientation determines the polysaccharide's properties"?

options.text: Linked genes
Central dogma
Missense mutation
Glycosidic bond

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Glycosidic bond".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A storage polysaccharide in plants made of α-glucose linked by α-1,4 (amylose) and α-1,4/α-1,6 (amylopectin) glycosidic bonds"?

options.text: Keystone species
Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle)
Starch
Energy coupling

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Starch".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A structural polysaccharide in plant cell walls made of β-glucose linked by β-1,4 glycosidic bonds; forms rigid, indigestible fibers"?

options.text: Cellulose
Hardy-Weinberg conditions
Endomembrane system
Molecular clock

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Cellulose".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A structural polysaccharide in arthropod exoskeletons and fungal cell walls; similar to cellulose but with nitrogen-containing groups"?

options.text: Alternative splicing
Chitin
Molecular clock
CRISPR-Cas9

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Chitin".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Carbohydrates: Structure and Function is accurate?

options.text: Cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs) activated by cyclins drive progression past checkpoints.

Translation occurs in three stages: initiation (ribosome assembles at start codon AUG), elongation (amino acids added one by one as ribosome moves along mRNA), and termination (release factor binds stop codon).
Cellular Respiration: Krebs Cycle (citric acid cycle) occurs in the mitochondrial matrix when pyruvate is converted to acetyl-CoA (producing CO₂ and NADH), which then enters the cycle.
Carbohydrates: Structure and Function range from simple sugars (monosaccharides like glucose, C₆H₁₂O₆) to complex polysaccharides (starch, glycogen, cellulose, chitin).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Carbohydrates: Structure and Function as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Carbohydrates: Structure and Function?

options.text: Interspecific competition (competition exclusion principle or resource partitioning), predation (including coevolutionary arms races), mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism shape community structure.
Environmental factors (temperature, pH, salt concentration) also affect enzyme activity.
Recombination frequency between linked genes (calculated from testcross offspring) indicates the distance between genes on a chromosome, measured in centimorgans (cM) or map units.
Monosaccharides join via glycosidic bonds through dehydration synthesis to form disaccharides and polysaccharides.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Carbohydrates: Structure and Function as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Carbohydrates: Structure and Function?

options.text: Saturated vs. unsaturated fatty acids differ in the presence of C=C double bonds, affecting fluidity and melting point.
Enzymes are typically proteins with a specific active site that binds substrates via the induced fit model.
The specific bond orientation (α vs. β linkages) determines digestibility and function: α-1,4 bonds in starch are digestible by humans, while β-1,4 bonds in cellulose are not.
The three modes of natural selection are directional (shifts the mean), stabilizing (reduces variation around the mean), and disruptive (favors extremes).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Carbohydrates: Structure and Function as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Carbohydrates: Structure and Function?

options.text: Carbohydrates: Structure and Function range from simple sugars (monosaccharides like glucose, C₆H₁₂O₆) to complex polysaccharides (starch, glycogen, cellulose, chitin).
Alternative splicing enables one gene to produce multiple protein variants.
In eukaryotes, pre-mRNA undergoes processing: 5' cap addition, 3' poly-A tail, and splicing to remove introns and join exons.
NADH and FADH₂ donate electrons to protein complexes (I-IV), which pump H⁺ from the matrix to the intermembrane space, creating a proton gradient.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Carbohydrates: Structure and Function as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Carbohydrates: Structure and Function?

options.text: Monosaccharides join via glycosidic bonds through dehydration synthesis to form disaccharides and polysaccharides.
Adaptive radiation (rapid diversification from a single ancestor into multiple ecological niches) often follows mass extinctions or colonization of new environments.
Phylogenies reveal the nested hierarchy of life and enable predictions about uncharacterized organisms.
Enzymes are typically proteins with a specific active site that binds substrates via the induced fit model.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Carbohydrates: Structure and Function as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Carbohydrates: Structure and Function?

options.text: Blood glucose regulation via insulin and glucagon controlling the interconversion of glucose and glycogen in liver and muscle cells
Whale evolution documented through a remarkable fossil series: Pakicetus (land mammal) → Ambulocetus (walking whale) → Dorudon (aquatic) → modern whales over ~50 million years
Colon cancer progression following the multistep model: APC loss → K-ras activation → DCC loss → p53 loss → metastatic cancer over 10-15 years
Detecting natural selection by comparing observed genotype frequencies to Hardy-Weinberg predictions and identifying significant deviations using chi-square tests

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Carbohydrates: Structure and Function.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Carbohydrates: Structure and Function?

options.text: Virus tracking during COVID-19 pandemic using SARS-CoV-2 genome sequencing to build phylogenetic trees tracing transmission chains and identifying new variants
ABO blood type compatibility in transfusions determined by codominant Iᴬ and Iᴮ alleles and recessive i allele (3 alleles, 6 genotypes, 4 phenotypes)
Cellulose in dietary fiber promoting digestive health despite being indigestible because humans lack cellulase enzymes
Galápagos finch adaptive radiation: a single ancestor species diversifying into 14 species with specialized beak shapes for different food sources within ~1-2 million years

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Carbohydrates: Structure and Function.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Carbohydrates: Structure and Function applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: ABO blood type compatibility in transfusions determined by codominant Iᴬ and Iᴮ alleles and recessive i allele (3 alleles, 6 genotypes, 4 phenotypes)
Chitin-based biomaterials used in medical sutures and wound dressings due to their biocompatibility and structural strength
Mitochondrial diseases caused by mutations in ETC complex genes, affecting energy-demanding tissues (brain, heart, muscles) and causing conditions like Leigh syndrome
Forensic genetics using Mendelian inheritance patterns to establish paternity or identify family relationships from DNA evidence

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Carbohydrates: Structure and Function.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Carbohydrates: Structure and Function?

options.text: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) caused by nondisjunction during meiosis, resulting in an extra copy of chromosome 21 in all cells
Cell membrane fluidity regulation through cholesterol insertion between phospholipids and adjustment of saturated vs. unsaturated fatty acid ratios
Biomagnification of mercury and DDT through food chains: top predators (eagles, tuna) accumulate the highest concentrations, explaining why large predatory fish have mercury advisories
Blood glucose regulation via insulin and glucagon controlling the interconversion of glucose and glycogen in liver and muscle cells

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Carbohydrates: Structure and Function.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Carbohydrates: Structure and Function in practice?

options.text: Antioxidant research examining whether dietary antioxidants (vitamins C, E) can reduce oxidative DNA damage from reactive oxygen species
Cellulose in dietary fiber promoting digestive health despite being indigestible because humans lack cellulase enzymes
Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer) caused by inherited mutations in mismatch repair genes, increasing cancer risk
Active transport of ions (Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase) using ATP to pump 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per cycle, maintaining the resting membrane potential essential for nerve impulses

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Carbohydrates: Structure and Function.
